quiz_questions.question_text: What is diffraction?

options.text: Restricting light oscillations to a single plane using a filter
Bouncing of light waves off a surface back into the same medium
Conversion of light energy into thermal energy within a material
Bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffraction is the bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings. It is most noticeable when the opening size is comparable to the wavelength.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is diffraction most significant?

options.text: When the opening is very large
At very high frequencies
In a vacuum
When the opening is comparable to the wavelength

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffraction is most significant when the size of the opening or obstacle is comparable to the wavelength of the wave. Much larger openings produce minimal diffraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Condition for bright fringes in Young's double slit:

options.text: d = nλ
d sin θ = (n+½)λ
d cos θ = nλ
d sin θ = nλ

quiz_questions.explanation: Bright fringes (constructive interference) occur when d sin θ = nλ, where n = 0, 1, 2... This is when the path difference is a whole number of wavelengths.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a diffraction grating?

options.text: A curved reflective surface that focuses light to a single point
A device with many parallel slits producing sharp bright maxima
A transparent filter that selectively transmits certain wavelengths
A single narrow opening used to produce a central bright fringe

quiz_questions.explanation: A diffraction grating has many closely spaced parallel slits. The large number of slits produces very sharp, well-defined bright maxima, useful for analyzing light spectra.

quiz_questions.question_text: In single-slit diffraction, which maximum is brightest?

options.text: The central maximum
The edges
The first side maximum
They are all equal

quiz_questions.explanation: In single-slit diffraction, the central maximum is the brightest and widest. Secondary maxima on either side are progressively dimmer and narrower.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for fringe spacing?

options.text: Δy = λL/d
Δy = dL/λ
Δy = λd/L
Δy = d/(λL)

quiz_questions.explanation: Fringe spacing Δy = λL/d, where λ is wavelength, L is the distance to the screen, and d is the slit separation. Wider spacing occurs with longer wavelengths or narrower slit separations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What must two sources be for interference to occur?

options.text: Coherent
Incoherent
Very bright
The same color only

quiz_questions.explanation: For a stable interference pattern, the two sources must be coherent — meaning they have the same frequency and a constant phase relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when two wave crests meet at the same point?

options.text: They cancel each other out completely
They combine to make a larger amplitude wave
They pass through without any interaction
They reflect off each other and reverse direction

quiz_questions.explanation: When two crests (or two troughs) meet, constructive interference occurs — the amplitudes add together, producing a wave with greater amplitude than either individual wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when a wave crest meets a wave trough?

options.text: The waves combine to produce a louder sound wave
The waves reflect off each other in opposite directions
The waves cancel each other out partially or completely
The waves merge permanently into a single combined wave

quiz_questions.explanation: When a crest meets a trough, destructive interference occurs. If the amplitudes are equal, they cancel completely, producing zero displacement. Otherwise, partial cancellation occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What produces the pattern of bright and dark fringes in Young's experiment?

options.text: Light reflecting off the edges of the slits themselves
Different colors of light separating through the slit gap
Constructive and destructive interference of light waves
Shadows cast by the barrier between the two narrow slits

quiz_questions.explanation: The bright fringes are where light from both slits arrives in phase (constructive interference), while dark fringes are where the light arrives out of phase (destructive interference).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'constructive interference'?

options.text: When waves combine to produce a wave with smaller amplitude
When waves combine to produce a wave with larger amplitude
When waves pass through each other without any change at all
When one wave completely absorbs the energy of another wave

quiz_questions.explanation: Constructive interference occurs when two waves are in phase (crests align with crests) and their amplitudes add together, producing a combined wave with larger amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'destructive interference'?

options.text: When waves combine and their amplitudes cancel each other out
When waves combine and produce a larger resulting amplitude
When a wave is destroyed by hitting a solid barrier surface
When light waves change into heat energy upon contact with matter

quiz_questions.explanation: Destructive interference occurs when two waves are out of phase (crest meets trough) and their amplitudes subtract, reducing or eliminating the combined wave amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'coherent' mean when describing light sources?

options.text: The sources have the same brightness and intensity level
The sources emit all colors of the visible spectrum equally
The sources have the same frequency and a constant phase difference
The sources are positioned at the same distance from a detector

quiz_questions.explanation: Coherent light sources have the same frequency and maintain a constant phase relationship over time. This is essential for producing a stable, observable interference pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'path difference' in the context of interference?

options.text: The difference in distance traveled by two waves from their sources to a point
The difference in speed between two waves in different media types
The difference in frequency between two waves from different sources
The difference in amplitude between two interfering wave crests

quiz_questions.explanation: Path difference is the difference in the distance each wave travels from its source to a given point. Whether interference is constructive or destructive depends on whether this difference is a whole number of wavelengths or a half-number.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by an 'interference pattern'?

options.text: A random scattering of light in all directions from a rough surface
A regular arrangement of bright and dark bands from overlapping waves
A single bright spot formed by focusing light through a lens system
A continuous spectrum of colors produced by a glass prism device

quiz_questions.explanation: An interference pattern is the regular arrangement of bright (constructive) and dark (destructive) bands or fringes produced when two or more coherent waves overlap.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'fringe' in an interference or diffraction pattern?

options.text: The edge of a shadow cast by an opaque straight barrier
A bright or dark band in the pattern caused by wave interaction
The border around a lens where light is blocked from passing
The central opening in a barrier through which waves pass

quiz_questions.explanation: A fringe is one of the alternating bright or dark bands in an interference or diffraction pattern. Bright fringes correspond to constructive interference and dark fringes to destructive interference.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'wavelength' mean?

options.text: The height of a wave measured from its rest position to its peak
The distance between two consecutive crests or troughs of a wave
The number of wave cycles that pass a point every second of time
The speed at which a wave moves through a particular medium

quiz_questions.explanation: Wavelength is the distance between two consecutive identical points on a wave, such as crest to crest or trough to trough. It is usually measured in meters and symbolized by λ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'monochromatic' mean when describing light?

options.text: Light that contains all visible colors mixed together equally
Light that consists of a single wavelength or color only
Light that has been polarized to vibrate in only one plane
Light that travels in perfectly parallel rays without spreading

quiz_questions.explanation: Monochromatic light consists of a single wavelength (and therefore a single color). Lasers produce highly monochromatic light, which is ideal for interference and diffraction experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'superposition' of waves?

options.text: When one wave completely replaces another wave at a point
When two waves overlap and their displacements add together
When a wave bounces off a barrier and reverses its direction
When a wave changes speed as it enters a different medium

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle of superposition states that when two or more waves overlap, the resulting displacement at any point is the sum of the individual displacements. This is the basis of both constructive and destructive interference.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'in phase' mean for two waves?

options.text: The waves have different frequencies but the same amplitude
The waves reach their crests and troughs at the same time
The waves travel in opposite directions through the medium
The waves have the same speed but different wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: Two waves are in phase when their crests and troughs align at the same time and place. This condition leads to constructive interference and maximum combined amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shines a laser through a narrow single slit and sees a pattern of bright and dark bands on a screen. What causes the dark bands?

options.text: The slit edges block some of the laser light from reaching the screen
Parts of the diffracted wave cancel out through destructive interference
The screen absorbs certain wavelengths of the laser light selectively
The laser beam splits into separate beams that miss parts of the screen

quiz_questions.explanation: In single-slit diffraction, light from different parts of the slit travels different distances to points on the screen. At certain angles, these waves are out of phase and cancel each other through destructive interference, creating dark bands.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student increases the slit separation in a double-slit experiment. What happens to the fringe spacing on the screen?

options.text: The fringe spacing increases because the slits are farther apart
The fringe spacing decreases because Δy = λL/d and d increased
The fringe spacing stays the same regardless of slit separation
The fringes disappear completely when the slits move farther apart

quiz_questions.explanation: Fringe spacing Δy = λL/d. Since d is in the denominator, increasing slit separation d decreases fringe spacing. The bright and dark bands become closer together.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses red light and then blue light in a double-slit experiment. How does the fringe pattern change?

options.text: Blue light produces wider spaced fringes because it has more energy
Blue light produces narrower spaced fringes because it has shorter wavelength
Both lights produce identical fringe patterns with the same spacing
Blue light produces no fringes because it cannot be diffracted at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Since Δy = λL/d, shorter wavelength (blue) produces smaller fringe spacing. Red light (longer wavelength) produces more widely spaced fringes than blue light.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that sound can be heard around corners but light cannot bend around corners in the same way. Why?

options.text: Sound is a wave but light is not a wave so it cannot diffract at all
Sound has much longer wavelengths comparable to doorway sizes causing more diffraction
Light travels too fast to bend around corners while sound is slow enough
Sound reflects off walls while light is absorbed by the wall material

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffraction is most significant when the wavelength is comparable to the opening size. Sound wavelengths (centimeters to meters) are similar to doorway sizes, so sound diffracts easily. Light wavelengths (hundreds of nanometers) are far too small for noticeable diffraction around everyday objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student views a CD under white light and sees rainbow-like colors. What optical phenomenon causes this?

options.text: The CD acts as a prism and refracts light through its plastic material
The CD surface acts as a diffraction grating separating wavelengths
The metallic coating on the CD filters out certain colors of light
The CD surface polarizes white light into separate color components

quiz_questions.explanation: The closely spaced grooves on a CD surface act like a diffraction grating. Different wavelengths of white light are diffracted at different angles, producing the rainbow pattern of colors.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student moves the screen farther from the double slits. What happens to the fringe spacing?

options.text: The fringe spacing increases because Δy = λL/d and L increased
The fringe spacing decreases because the screen is farther away
The fringe spacing stays exactly the same at every screen distance
The fringes disappear entirely when the screen is moved too far away

quiz_questions.explanation: Since Δy = λL/d, increasing the screen distance L increases the fringe spacing proportionally. The bright and dark bands spread farther apart.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a diffraction grating to analyze light from a gas lamp. They observe distinct colored lines. What does this tell them about the gas?

options.text: The gas is extremely hot and emitting white light at all wavelengths
The gas emits light at specific wavelengths unique to its composition
The grating creates artificial colors that are not actually in the light
The gas absorbs all colors except the ones seen through the grating

quiz_questions.explanation: Each element emits light at characteristic wavelengths. The diffraction grating separates these wavelengths into distinct lines, allowing identification of the gas — this is the basis of spectroscopy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student designs noise-canceling headphones. Which wave principle do they rely on?

options.text: Constructive interference to amplify desired sounds only
Destructive interference to cancel out unwanted noise waves
Diffraction to bend sound waves away from the listener's ears
Refraction to redirect noise waves around the headphone casing

quiz_questions.explanation: Noise-canceling headphones use microphones to detect ambient noise and produce sound waves that are out of phase (inverted) with the noise. When these waves overlap, destructive interference reduces or eliminates the unwanted sound.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that when they make the single slit narrower, the central bright band gets wider. Why?

options.text: A narrower slit lets more light through, so the band spreads out more
A narrower slit causes greater diffraction and more spreading of waves
A narrower slit focuses the light beam into a broader parallel beam
A narrower slit changes the wavelength of the light passing through

quiz_questions.explanation: When the slit width decreases and becomes more comparable to the wavelength, diffraction effects increase. Greater diffraction causes the light to spread out more, resulting in a wider central maximum.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sets up Young's double-slit experiment and counts 10 bright fringes on the screen. If they switch from green light to red light, what changes?

options.text: More fringes fit on the screen because red light has a shorter wavelength
Fewer fringes fit on the screen because red light has a longer wavelength
The same number of fringes appear because color does not affect spacing
No fringes appear because red light cannot produce interference patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: Red light has a longer wavelength than green light, so it produces wider fringe spacing (Δy = λL/d). With wider spacing, fewer fringes fit on the same screen.
